inlineContent.text: , which implements the matrix representation of rotations. Unlike
, which stores rotation, scale, and shearing, quaternions only store rotation.

Quaternions can be parametrized using both an axis-angle pair or Euler angles. Due to their compactness and the way they are stored in memory, certain operations (obtaining axis-angle and performing SLERP, in particular) are more efficient and robust against floating-point errors.
Quaternions need to be normalized before being used for rotation.

matrix. If a vector is transformed by an identity quaternion, it will not change.

Returns the negative value of the Quaternion. This is the same as writing Quaternion(-q.x, -q.y, -q.z, -q.w). This operation results in a quaternion that represents the same rotation.

Returns the quaternion’s rotation in the form of Euler angles. The Euler order depends on the
parameter, for example using the YXZ convention: since this method decomposes, first Z, then X, and Y last. See the
enum for possible values. The returned vector contains the rotation angles in the format (X angle, Y angle, Z angle).
